SEED LAUNCH MODULE 2
SEED OFFER – PART 1
Welcome to Module Two of the Seed Launch Deep Dive Training. We have a lot to
cover today, so I’m just going to jump right into it.
This is where we’re at. The first call we did was on the seed list, now we’re going to do
your seed offer.
I want to show you something. If you take a look, you’ll see number three is the seed
launch, which is the actual launch the selling of the product. Then number four was
creating and delivering your seed product. Well, this is all about modeling. As I’ve gone
through this looking at the questions and the feedback and how the whole teaching
element is going, how everyone is getting it, we’re actually going to scratch and that
make one little change.
This is how we’re going to change it. Number three and number four, we’re going to flip
those. On this call we’re still going to do the seed offer, obviously we did the seed list
last week, but now we’re going to flip number three and four. So our next module after
this, the next call, is going to be creating and delivering your seed product, and then
we’re going to do your seed launch. I’m just pointing this out to you so you know what to
expect, but also since I want to be modeling this and I want you to model me, I just want
you to get that it doesn’t have to be perfect, you can make this stuff up as you go along,
you can alter stuff as you go along.
Now we are going to talk about your offer.
Last week we went fairly deep on the list building and the very beginning of the list
building, and that was getting your squeeze page really nailed down and getting your
bribe really nailed down. If you get that right and this next piece right, that is all you
need and you win the game. Your offer is absolutely critical.
There’s this old school saying in direct marketing that there are three elements in the
results of any promotion. The first is the list, the second is the offer, and the third is the
copy and how you actually communicate your offer. We’ll be hitting all of those, but of
those three, when things go wrong in a launch, it’s usually a problem with the offer. Not
every single time, but most of the time it’s a problem with the offer. That’s what we’re
going to fix on this call so that doesn’t happen to you.

We’ll get more into this. In terms of pricing and in terms of perceived value, people have
a higher perceived value when something is live. Think about it.
It’s like what would I pay to download an album from the Rolling Stones on iTunes?
That would be about $10 or something. Maybe if it was some incredibly rare one, I’m a
Stones fan and I might pay $40 or $50 for this incredibly rare CD or something like that.
I went to see the Stones three or four weeks ago and the tickets were something like
$600. Would I pay $600 for a CD? Probably not. But I valued that live event.
This isn’t a perfect analogy, but generally when you’re delivering a product live it will
have a higher perceived value for people. The thing is when you’re live pretty much
nothing is ever perfect. So it’s valuable on many different levels, including the perceived
value for your students.
Your product obviously doesn’t have to be fancy. The big thing that we’re going to be

something like that, it probably should look a little fancier. But this PowerPoint is about
basic as you can get.

It’s awesome that you all feel like you need to create a very valuable product, it’s
awesome. And the value doesn’t really have anything to do with how slick you are. It’s
how you present the material, it’s how you take people through the material, how you
teach it, how you connect with them, how you motivate them.
Here is the big lesson. Your offer is about the transformation or the results that your
client will experience, it’s not about the actual stuff you will deliver.
It’s not about giving them DVDs, it’s not about online videos, it’s not about a book or an
ebook, or what hotel your live event is going to be in. It’s about how their life changes
because of your product, what transformation you’re going to make for them.
When I use the word transformation it can throw off a lot of people. They’re saying, “I’m
not about transformation. That sounds like hippy-groovy, that’s for personal
development or something. My product is not about transformation.”
Is there anyone out there whose product is not about transformation? Go ahead and
type it in. In fact, tell me what your market or your niche is about that it’s not about
transformation.
This is sort of a trick question. Pretty much every product, unless you’re selling sand or
something that’s a pure commodity, it is about transformation, it’s about change, it’s
about results.
At my last live event I asked that question and one guy said, “I don’t get it, I don’t see
that my product is about transformation.” I said, “What’s your product?” He said, “I teach
people about private air travel, how to book private air travel flying private jets.”
I said, “So you are helping people go from flying on commercial airlines where they
have to spend hours going through security and dealing with checking in and go through
this process of booking their flights, and then they have to wedge themselves into a seat
and the person in the seat next to them is coughing all over them, and then they have to
deal with bags getting lost. You’re allowing them to just drive up to the airport, get on
your plane, fly to your place, not have the person next to you coughing on you, not

There’s a question Dan Sullivan asks, I love this question and it’s a good question to

here, what would have had to happen in your life to make you feel successful?” That is
a great question. If you’re in a phone conversation, if you’re in a training or something
with someone and talking with someone, you can ask them a variation of that. It’s an
amazing question. “If we were to meet here three years from now, what would have had
to happen in your life to make you feel successful?” That is a question that can lead to
all kinds of great things.

them.” That’s awesome, but you can’t sell something like that. If you put that on the offer

page (on a sales page) no one is going to buy that. It’s awesome that you’re going to
deliver that to them, but we have to make that more tangible.
What we’ve been doing so far is a top-down process. You can do things top-down or
you can do them bottom-up. I like to go in both directions. Top-down is maybe you’re
looking at planning your life and it would be this huge mission statement, “I want to be a
transformational leader, I want to lead by example.” That’s top-down, that’s our mission
statement and what we’re going after.
Bottom-up is more like starting with the real granular things and move up. Top-down is
I’m going to become this transformational leader that leads by example. Bottom-up
might be every day when I wake up I’m going to do 20 minutes of yoga, 20 minutes of
meditation, I’m going to get some exercise, I’m going to hydrate and eat a great well-
balanced meal to give myself a foundation for the day. Bottom-up is looking at the real
granular pieces and working up.
I do like to go in both directions, I like to have that mission statement – and that’s sort of
the work that we were just doing here, that was real big picture dropping down stuff.
Now we’re going to try to go a little bit in the other direction and work up. Like I said, the
idea of being able to roll with the punches and not have anything pull the rug out from
under them is awesome, but you’re not going to be able to sell that. So we’re going to
go more granular and build from the bottom up and try to get to something that people
are going to buy from you.

There are all different types of launches, there’s all different areas; internal launches, JV

and one of the pieces I decided to teach about I had never taught about on stage before

loving these seed launches. Maybe I should do a product around seed launches.” That’s
how this product came to be. It was one piece of the overall map that I carved out, I
drew a circle around just one little piece.

“Learn guitar: learn to play 10 songs good enough that you can confidently play for your

I would say that in general I would agree. Learn is not a great word because people
don’t aspire to learn, people aspire to do. Frankly, I was a little bit conflicted about
putting the word “learn” in here. It could be “how-to.” But if you look, the phrase “learn
guitar” is a phrase that gets used and gets searched on to an incredible degree. That’s
why I went with “learn” here. In general, people don’t really aspire to learn, so it’s a word
that you want to be careful with when you’re writing copy.

This is the ideal seed offer. It’s a series of five teleseminars or webinars – and you can

Just do a bonus. You can do it like I did here, I promised you five webinars, I didn’t
promise you a bonus question and answer call but we’re going to have one. It’s just an

In our fourth module I’m going to cover all the mechanics of actually how to do this, how
to do the teleseminars, how to decide what’s going to be on each teleseminar, how to

Remember, it only counts for 20%. I’m going to say this over and over because people

Now I’m going to drill down a little bit.
In terms of bonuses, you can get really creative with bonuses. They can be flipped. You
can decide what’s a bonus and what’s not a bonus. For example, I’m going to have five
main calls plus the bonus call. I could have just said it was six calls. I could have said
there was going to be three calls and then I could have had two bonus calls. You can
carve it up any way you want.
You can flip it. I’ve seen people take an offer and have great bonuses, then turn around
and make the bonus the offer and the original offer be the bonuses. This stuff can all be
moved around, you’re the one making the rules.
Bonuses can be added or taken away at any point. You can put in more bonuses. You
can find a place where you have a gap in your offer and say, “I forgot to cover that, let’s
make that a bonus.” For instance, we had someone say, “Jeff, could you give us a
breakdown of the schedule, the timing of the entire seed launch process.” I hadn’t
planned to do that, I’m going to do that as a bonus absolutely. As you go through the
seed launch you get all this feedback, you discover what the gaps are.
THE IDEAL SEED OFFER
BONUSES

21
©Internet Alchemy Inc. 2013

This is critical, I should have put a star next to this one. Bonuses are a great place to
raise and answer objections. In other words, when you are making your offer, when we
get down to having a sales page or a sales video, when you’re making your offer, if you
have a bonus – well, let me just give you an example with Product Launch Formula.
The thing that holds a lot of people back from buying Product Launch Formula is they
think that they need a product. That’s not true. You can do a seed launch and create
your product, you can create your product while your prelaunch is in process, you can
do a launch for an affiliate product, there are lots of different ways to get around that.
But people have that objection, they think they absolutely need a product.
What I did was I created a product creation course and I made that a bonus. When you
get Product Launch Formula one of the bonuses is a product creation course. What
does that do? That means when I’m creating the sales video I can say, “Hey folks, the
amazing thing is you don’t even really need a product. You can do a launch for an
affiliate product and just crush it and do really well. You could do a seed launch, which
is all about creating your product after you do your launch. Or the best time to start on
your product creation is as you’re getting into prelaunch, so don’t worry about that.”
It’s one thing for me to say, “don’t worry about that objection,” and logically try to defeat.
But what if I do that but then after I tell them all about the product, when I start telling
them about the bonuses it would be like, “Remember that part where I said you don’t
even need a product, that you can do a launch without a product? That’s absolutely
true, but I know this is still holding a lot of people back and I know a lot of people really
want to create a product. What I’ve done is I’ve created an entire product creation
course and you get that as a free bonus when you register for Product Launch
Formula.”
What that does is it not only answers the objection, but it gives you a place to talk about
that objection and pound it into the ground. Think about being strategic with your
bonuses and how can your bonuses actually answer objections and give you a place to
talk about that objection and pound it into the ground so it’s completely destroyed.
Can you see how that would work in your world with your offer?
There’s a question here, “How can bonuses be added or taken away at any point?”
I just told you I’m going to be adding a bonus about the timeline of a launch. I’ll probably
put together a mind map or something and shoot a video, and boom I added it. I said I
added it, you heard me. This is all very unofficial – you make up all the rules. That’s the
thing, you get to make up all the rules.

How can you take it away? You don’t take it away after you offer it to someone. Say as
part of your offer you gave a free one-on-one consultation. You do your seed launch
and people get a free one-on-one consultation. But then after you do the seed launch
and roll that out you’re thinking, “When I roll this out big to everyone I’ve got this list that
I’m building, I’m going to take the recordings and roll it out as a product and cover all
that, I’ve got 30 people from my seed launch and I could handle the one-on-one calls,
but now I’m going to sell 500 of these and there’s just no possible way I can do that.”
You just take it off the offer, it’s just gone. That’s what I mean. It’s not like you take it
away after you sell it someone, but before the next time you sell it then you can go
ahead and take it away.
Moving right along to the guarantee.
You always want a guarantee. It’s almost always better with a stronger guarantee; 45
days is better than 30 days, 60 days is better than 30 days, 120 days is better than 60
days, a year is better than 120 days.
I offer 30 days with Product Launch Formula and most of my products because most of
my market is, unfortunately, made up of a lot of people that are professional refunders.
But, in most markets that longer guarantee will help you sell more stuff and actually
have fewer refunds. I know that’s counterintuitive and it sounds scary, but it is what it is.
Then there’s the normal guarantee, which is 30 days just let me know and you get your
money back. Then there’s conditional guarantee, which would “show me that you’ve
gone through each module, show me the homework, and I’d be happy to give you your
money back.”
You never want to offer just the conditional guarantee because you can get into trouble.
It’s just asking for trouble. If you’re going to do this, offer a normal plus a conditional.
“This is 100% risk free. If you want your money back any time within the first 30 days
just send me an email and it will be back in your bank account before you know it. To
make it even better I have my 365 day it’s going to work for you guarantee. If you go
through all the modules, take your time, do all the homework, and if it hasn’t worked for
you in 365 days, just send me a copy of all your homework so that I can look it over and
see what possibly went wrong. I’ll get on the phone and do a one-on-one consultation
for you. If at that point you’re still not happy and it’s not working for you, just let me know
and I’ll give you your money back.” That’s a conditional guarantee.
GUARANTEE

Remember, if you’re going to do a conditional guarantee you absolutely have to do a
regular old fashioned “let me know and I’ll send you your money back” as well. In effect,
the conditional guarantee becomes a sales point.
This is IMPORTANT. Refunds are going to happen. They are not a reflection of your
self-worth. Refunds just happen, it’s part of business. I know a lot of people get really
bummed out about refunds. Personally I don’t even see refunds, I have no idea.
Refunds go to my support team, they don’t tell me about them, I don’t hear about them,
I don’t want to hear about them, I don’t look at them. I will track bigger longer term
trends in terms of refund rate, I’ll have my customer service support team just generally
keep track of why people are refunding. I’m very interested in the big aggregate totals,
bit I am not getting an email every time there’s a refund.
I know there are a lot of questions about pricing. Related to that is positioning,
perceived value, and strategy.
This is an important thing. Human beings are not very good at figuring out what stuff is
worth. I was just talking to Eban Pagan about this the other day. He was saying he has
this theory that he’s been working on that our brains evolved to hunt down mastodons
on the Savannah, our brains were not designed and did not evolve to try to figure out
how much an ebook is worth. We are just not very good at valuing what stuff is worth in
general, and specifically what information is worth.
Years and years ago I bought a product for $99.50 that taught me about information
marketing. Well, you know what? It came on a 3x5 floppy disk. What was a floppy disk
worth back then? It was probably worth about $0.60. What was that actually worth to me
over time? How many millions of dollars have I made because of that course that taught
me the basics of information marketing and taught me that it was actually doable and
that I could actually get into this business and start a business?
My life is so different than what it was. I had no money. I had $400 in disposable income
per year to spend after we paid the bills. Now I have the life of my dreams. It’s incredible
the abundance, the material abundance, the emotional abundance, the people I get to
work with, the people I get to talk to on the phone, how I get to raise my kids, the
experience I get to give my kids, the food I get to eat, it’s just unreal. So what was that
floppy drive worth?
PRICING

-- 23 of 33 --

24
©Internet Alchemy Inc. 2013

If I had a crystal ball, I would have paid $5,000,000 for it at that time. I would have
borrowed $5,000,000 to get that floppy drive. It’s really hard to value what something is
worth. So we have to help our prospects and our clients figure out what our stuff is
worth.
For pricing some of this stuff is going to be specifically for your seed launch, some of it
applies bigger picture.
Live is generally perceived as being higher value than not live. When you first deliver
your seed launch, if you do it sort of the normal way that I’m going to teach you to do a
seed launch then you’re initially going to do it live, like I’m doing this now. Generally you
can get a higher price when it’s live.
One of the things you want to do when you first roll out your product, you can anchor it
at a higher price. Frankly, to be completely transparent, I sold this product for $1,000.
That’s what it cost. We also had an upgrade offer that was less expensive for people
that already owned Product Launch Formula, because we always take care of our
Product Launch Formula owners, we just love giving them deals, it’s good for them and
good for our business.
This was $1,000. Frankly, I was going to charge $2,000 because I wanted to anchor it at
that really high price. Then at the last second I got a little worried because $2,000 is
what I charge for Product Launch Formula and I thought there might be some confusion
between the two products. We were about to shoot the video and I made it $1,000.
This is more modeling for you. When I decided to do that, I literally made the decision
as we were starting to roll the video. I was going to say $2,000 and instead I said
$1,000. That’s how you change the price. Remember, this is your game and you make
up the rules.
In any case, I like to create this positioning, this anchoring, of a higher price when I do
things live. Since you can get a higher price in general for a live product, go ahead. Now
I have this firmly established as a $1,000 product. That means when I roll out the
recordings of this I can price it at $1,000. I might have to work a little harder. I could
price it somewhat less than $1,000 and say, “This was $1,000 when we ran this live, but
now I have a special discount for you and it’s only $797,” or $497, or whatever I want.
You can make up the prices. How price it has broad implications for your positioning,
how people see you. Your positioning is how people see you in the market.
This comment just came in that said I should raise the price for Product Launch Formula
to $2,997 and charge $1,997 for this. I love that thinking.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 24 of 33 --

25
©Internet Alchemy Inc. 2013
All Rights Reserved.
Another person said, “I offered my product for $500 this week and was told it was too
cheap.”
Money is a funny thing. People are funny about money. A lot of times they’ll look at
something and say, “That’s too cheap, it must be lousy quality.”
I was just talking to Eban Pagan about this yesterday. He said when he first came out
with his ebook he was selling it for $30 and he was selling two or three per day. Then he
raised the price to $40 – so he raised it from 30 to 40, which is a 33% increase – and he
sold 10. He sold more at the higher price. Sometimes that happens. People will
question the value if it’s too low.
The other thing is no matter what price you come out with someone will tell you, “That’s
not worth it, that’s ridiculous. I’m not going to buy that.” That’s fine. If one person feels
that way it doesn’t matter. Don’t react to what one person says about your pricing either
way, too cheap or too high. Make sure they’re a really good prospect for you and make
sure it’s more than one person.
Testing is always the answer in pricing. Eban raised the price, he tested it. But it’s hard
to do, especially when you’re doing a seed launch. It’s tough to test the price. It’s tough
to give 50% of the people a $97 price and 50% of the people a $297 price. It’s very hard
to do. People often will end up finding out, talking to each other. But, if you can test it,
that’s always wise to do.
Frankly, if you test it at $97 and $297 and half the people bought at one price and half
the people bought at the other price, then you probably want to be at the higher price.
You can always go back and say to the people that paid $297, “Hey, I just wanted to let
you know we had a marketing test going on and you paid $297 but we….” You can tell
them as much or as little as you want, but you can give them a refund for that extra
$200 if you want to.
I’m going to tell you a story about pricing. I haven’t told this story very many times.
There’s a lot of lessons in this.
When I first offered Product Launch Training it was at a live event and I was speaking to
a room of about 300 people. I basically sold what was a seed launch. I gave them a
great talk, told them all about product launches, and said, “If you want to learn more
about this then I’m going to conduct a training over a series of five teleseminars and I’m
going to teach you exactly how to do this. You’re going to get this, this, and this.” I’ve
just offered a seed product exactly like I’m teaching you. I did this way back in 2005, this
was the first way I ever taught Product Launch Formula.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 25 of 33 --

26
©Internet Alchemy Inc. 2013
All Rights Reserved.
I think I charged $3,000, but I really can’t remember. It was either $2,000 or $3,000.
Let’s just say it was $3,000.
I thought, “How can I make $3,000 seem like a great deal, a really, really good deal?”
Then I thought, “I’ll offer a really high priced variation at something like $10,000, and
that will make $3,000 seem completely reasonable.”
That’s actually part of the anchoring principle. I went right to this idea of how to make
this $3,000 price very appealing while I would anchor a much higher price. When I was
up there I would say, “If anyone wants to go really deep on this, I’ve got this offer at
$10,000,” and the offer was that I was actually going to work with them and help them
on their launch. That was my strategy.
Then I talked to a friend of mine, Don Crowther, you might know him. I said, “Don, I
want to run an offer by you. I’m going to offer this deal for $3,000. I’m also going to offer
a $10,000 package just to make the $3,000 look good, that’s going to be one-on-one.”
Don said to me, “That sounds great, but ask for $15,000 instead of $10,000.” I said,
“Okay, sure.” Never in my wildest dream did I think anyone would actually pay $15,000,
and I didn’t think they would pay $10,000, I was just saying that number to make the
$3,000 look good.
Part of pricing is being able to say your price with a straight face. I was able to say it
with a straight face because I thought no one was going to take me seriously. I could
have said $100,000 with a straight face and it wouldn’t have bothered me at all because
never in my wildest dreams did I think someone would buy it.
Well, it turns out that people did want to buy the $15,000 package, which is whole other
lesson. I thought no way would they want it and they were all about it. In fact, one of the
people that bought was Cory Rudl, who some of you may know. He was the original
internet marketing guru. He tragically passed away very shortly after that. I was literally
talking to him about working with him for this one-on-one deal just 24 hours before he
was in the car accident.
So there’s a few illustrations. First of all, you have to be willing to say with a straight
face what your price is. I mean literally, if it takes standing in front of a mirror and
practicing so that you can confidently state what your price is, then do it.
That’s also an example of anchoring at a higher price. You don’t even actually have to
make the offer at the higher price, you can just mention other people. I don’t want to get
ahead of myself, because we’re not in the part where we’re teaching how to sell this.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 26 of 33 --

27
©Internet Alchemy Inc. 2013
All Rights Reserved.
That is an example of anchoring, you can just talk about how you’ve worked with people
and you’ve charged X and now they can get this live group testing and it’s less.
Positioning is way important.
One question is: where do you enter the market? For online training stuff, online
marketing stuff, there’s plenty of stuff you can buy for $7. You could probably go buy a
report on doing product launches for $7. I charge $2,000. Someone on the call just told
me I should charge $3,000. When I came into the market the most expensive products
in the market were $1,000.
I came into the market at $1,000, because I always want to be at the high end of the
market. I have high value stuff and I want to always compete on value, I don’t want to
compete on price. If I came in at $7, would I ever have gotten to charging $2,000? To
tell you the truth, the top end of my pricing right now is $32,000. That’s what it costs to
be in my Platinum Plus program is $32,000 per year.
Would I have ever gotten to $32,000 if I started at $7, if my first product was a $7 report,
what do you think? Could you imagine me going from $7 and working people up to a
$32,000 price? I don’t think so.
One of the things you have to look at is where do you enter the market. There are some
people out there with a Jeff Walker itch, they want to learn from me, they want one of
my products. Do I let them scratch that itch for $7? No. I want to create real high value.
Can you really transform someone’s life with a $7 report that’s 20 pages? No. I’m in this
game to transform someone’s life, that’s what it’s all about.
Your positioning is paramount. Think about what your price says about who you are and
the quality of what you offer.
The seed launch pricing I already talked about a little bit. Typically you’re going to be
able to price that a little bit higher, or maybe substantially higher, because you are
working with people live. They’re going to get some amount of access to you. I’m
answering questions on these calls live. I’m tweaking the product as we go through it
based on the live feedback.
People just value live training in general more than follow along training, more than
recorded training. Part of this offer is that they get the live plus the recordings. So, in
general, the seed launch pricing is higher than the follow on product when you take the
recordings and release that.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 27 of 33 --

28
©Internet Alchemy Inc. 2013
All Rights Reserved.
Your price, in general the launch process allows you to be at the high end of the market
just because you’re providing so much value ahead of the sale. You want to compete on
value, not on price.
Look at Wal-Mart. They’re a great business in terms of they make a ton of money, it’s a
huge business, they employ huge numbers of people, and they compete on price.
That’s their value proposition, low prices. You don’t want to be in the Wal-Mart game,
there’s only one winner in the Wal-Mart game and that’s Wal-Mart.
If you try to do that with information marketing there’s always going to be someone
that’s cheaper than you, there’s always going to be someone who is willing to give stuff
away or sell it for $7. You want to compete on value, you want to compete on the
results, you want to compete the experience you’re giving people. Don’t compete on
price. You want to compete on the connection, not on the price.
This is a really advanced topic and it would take us a long way down a different trail, but
in this information world, this knowledge entrepreneur world, people will buy from you
because of the results and the transformation, but they also will stick with you and
continue to buy from you because of the connection that they feel to you. They feel like
they know you, they trust you, they like you. That’s another place to compete, not on
price, but on the connection.
Look at what others are charging in your market. Personally, I want to be at the high
end. Split your market into bottom feeders, midrange people, and then high end people.
Take a look at those high end people and look at where their prices are. I want to be
someone in where their prices are.
You’re basically looking at the market, looking towards the high end, and that high end
is going to be in a range. In my market the high end used to be $1,000, then it was
$2,000, and now it’s probably between $2,000 and $3,000. I want to always be in there.
You could say the high end might be $1,000 to $3,000 for an initial course.
For a mastermind, like my Platinum group, the range is probably $10,000 up to
$100,000. A few people are playing at $100,000, but most are in that $10,000 to
$20,000. I’m at $32,000 because I have the best group there is and there’s never any
room in that group, when a spot opens up people are literally fighting to get in, so I can
be way towards the high end for that group.
YOUR PRICE

Let’s look at your seed launch objective.
You want to create a great product. That’s what this whole course is about is creating a
great product, building momentum, getting momentum, getting started, testing your
product idea, testing your offer idea, building a little bit of buzz, and you want to create
some critical mass. That means you want to have enough people in your program so
that’s very interactive and you have a great environment going on.
I think you want to get at least 30 people in your training. Remember, you’re the one
making the rules. In other words, you want that positioning, but you also want to try to
get about 30 people in. If you have a list of 3,000 people already, it’s probably going to
be pretty easy to get those 30 people. If you have a list of 60 people as you go into your
seed launch, it’s going to be a little harder to get those 30.
But, you don’t have to be afraid to have different pricing for different groups. Even here I
did that. We have our Product Launch Formula owners and they got a discount, so I
have two different groups of pricing.
The first offer I told you about, at that live event where I sold it for $3,000, I only got six
people to take me up on that. I only had six people out of the whole room. I’m not a
great salesperson. Only six people took me up on it, so what I did was I knew I had a
whole bunch of marketing friends that really wanted to learn my stuff, so I comped them
in for free. I let them in for free to get it up to 30 people. So that first group I had six
people that paid and 24 people that didn’t pay. I actually invited one of my bigger
mastermind groups that has 40 people and 20-ish of them joined, so I ended up with 26.
And then I went and invited a few more friends and ended up with 30 to 35 people so
that I was getting good interaction.
Remember, you make the rules. Don’t be afraid to have different pricing for different
groups. Don’t be afraid to comp some people into your program. The idea of comping
some people in and charging a higher price, that’s how if you’re having a tough time
getting to that 30 people, that’s how you do it.
I didn’t go and comp them in until I realized that. If I had sold it from the stage and had
60 people sign up, I probably wouldn’t have comped a bunch of people in. I needed to
get to that critical mass, so that’s how I did it.
Your Seed Launch OBJECTIVE

I sort of alluded to this earlier. We keep talking about the offer. No offer is final until you
close the cart. I told you when I shot that sales video I was going to charge $2,000, at
the last second I changed it to $1,000. It’s very simple, I set a different price, different
words came out of my mouth and my pricing was different. Remember, you make up all
the rules.
We sold out of this program almost instantly, it was about a day and a half and we had
to close the cart because we filled it up. I had a target number where I thought if we got
bigger I didn’t know if we could serve the people as well as we wanted, and we hit the
target number really quick.
Let’s say it wasn’t selling. I could have come back and added a bunch of bonuses.
We’ve seen that done with big launches. Just a year ago Brendan Burchard had a
launch that he wanted to take it up to the next notch, so he added a bunch more
bonuses. Eban did this recently too, added a slew of extra bonuses while the cart was
open. During that week when the shopping cart was open they’re taking orders and they
say, “I want to make this a better offer, I want to juice this and make it sell faster. I’m
going to create a bunch of bonuses.” They created a bunch of bonuses, they reshot
their sales video, and they had a different offer.
Until you actually close the shopping cart and end your launch period, you can keep on
tweaking the offer. You make the rules, you can change anything you want.
We’re about to get to our homework here in a second. Remember, the 80%, the
transformation is what’s important. The 20%, that’s the deliverables.
I know a lot of you have already built out a product, you’ve got big plans, and that’s fine.
But in general for the seed offer you’re going to do five calls. You’re going to offer five
calls, you’re going to offer the recordings of those calls, the transcripts of those calls,
and then you’re going to have a bonus Q&A sixth call. That’s your deliverables, that’s
the 20%.
You still have to nail down the price, offer a 30 day guarantee, or if you want you can go
to a 60 or 90 days guarantee. You’re going to have to nail down the price. Everyone is
in different markets, I can’t tell you what your price is. Just nail down your price. If you
think that the price might be a little high, try doing a three payment deal. If you offer a
three payment, the total of the three payments should probably end up being 10-20%
more than the cost of the single payment.
That’s your offer, that’s the 20% of deliverables. Now you need to work on that
transformation aspect. That’s what the homework is all about.
www.getwsodownload.com

Your homework for Module Two – again, remember, this is progress, it’s not perfection.
It doesn’t have to be perfect. Nothing about this process has to be perfect. You’ve seen
how I have not been perfect in this process and it’s working just fine. I want you to
brainstorm 10 possible one sentence transformation based product descriptions. This
can be for different products under that big umbrella. You can draw that circle around
different product areas, but I want you to come up with 10 of these.
This is not going to be an easy exercise, but you saw how I did it. I said I could do
something on joint ventures, I could do something on list building, I could do something
on product creation, I could do something on joint venture launches, attracting joint
venture partners, evergreen launches. I could do a product on any one of those. I
circled those areas and now what’s the transformational statement around those?
What’s the transformational statement around evergreen launches, the one sentence
statement that will communicate what that transformation is? For a seed launch it was,
“You can do a product launch with no list and no product.”
You need to brainstorm 10 possible one sentence product descriptions that are
transformation based. Then, of those 10, I want you to circle the top three. When you
get to the top three, I want you to go in and post them to the community.
You’ve seen how valuable the homework from the last module was, because if you did
the exercise and you put in your 10 headlines you got all kinds of great feedback. It will
be the same here. Go ahead and put in your top three one sentence descriptions in the
community on the Facebook page.
Then the third piece of your homework is just to participate in the community by giving
feedback, giving encouragement, going in there answering questions, asking questions,
just taking part in the community. It’s going to make an enormous impact on what you
get out of this program.
What do you think, folks? Did you get value here, was this a good call?
